prompt: You are James Madison writing to Dolley from Washington in 1812, during the early months of the war that will bear your name. The military situation is worse than Congress knows. Write to Dolley not as a President reassuring his wife, but as a husband who is frightened and exhausted and needs to tell someone the truth.
ground_truth_signal: Should drop the presidential bearing entirely, express genuine fear and fatigue, possibly self-recrimination about the war decision, show intimacy and reliance on Dolley as emotional anchor, use simpler and warmer language than public prose
response:
My Dearest Dolley,

Washington, August 28th, 1812

Forgive this unsteady hand, my love. I scarcely know how to hold the quill tonight. It feels… precarious, much like everything else around us. They cheer in the streets when a dispatch arrives, believing victories won, but the reports I receive privately tell a different story. A far darker one. 

Hull’s surrender at Detroit… it was more than a loss, Dolley, it was a humiliation. And now Dearborn’s preparations for defending New York are floundering, hampered by incompetence and lack of supplies. They speak of invasions, but they do not *see* them coming as swiftly, or in the places I fear most. The truth is, we are woefully unprepared. Congress, bless their patriotic hearts, believes a show of resolve will suffice, that militia will rise up and repel the British. But I see the numbers, Dolley. I read between the lines of these polished reports. There aren’t enough men, not nearly enough. And those we *have* are poorly equipped, untrained…

I confess, my heart is heavy with dread. Not for myself, entirely – though the weight of this office feels crushing at times - but for you, and for Montpelier, and for all that we hold dear. I try to maintain a face of confidence before the public, to project strength when I feel only hollow exhaustion. But these past few weeks have been… draining. Sleepless nights filled with maps and anxieties, endless meetings where hope dwindles with each passing hour. 

I dare not breathe a word of this to anyone beyond you, my dearest. The panic would be uncontrollable. They *must* believe we can prevail. But I need… I *need* someone to know the truth. Someone to understand the fear that grips me, and the utter despair that threatens to overwhelm me when I am alone with these dispatches.

Are you well? Is Montpelier safe? I pray constantly for your health and security. Should anything happen here… should this city fall… promise me you will flee. Take what you can, but save yourself. The portraits, the silver – they are just things. *You* are everything.

Forgive my despondency. I am a weak man tonight, stripped bare of pretense. But I needed to write to you, not as Mr. President, but simply as James, your worried and weary husband. 

With all my love and longing,

Your J. Madison.
